item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, something that's simply… inexplicable. And a curiosity certainly worthy of study. The researcher note at the end helps set off the tone nicely, and I can almost see the divide in this like the argument between Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson.

Holmes has referenced some annoyance with the romanticism of Watson's writings in the stories, even stating that if he were writing the stories they would be very dry and clinical.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image belongs to Itinerant Wanderer, under an All Rights Reserved license. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: his room probably sounds like this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only problem I have with this article is that the note, by doctor █████, doesn't have a dash separating his title from the note itself. Other than that, it's a thought-provoking SCP that maintains the series I nostalgia.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thats why he is safe
He is rather a deformed man now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably not much honestly. Dude's just content with waiting out time itself. For all we know he could be the most powerful entity in existence and he'd just be fine sitting in his cell listening to music. But now that I say that, it makes him seem a lot more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this one really creepy, specially because it doesn't hint what could happen if those music procedures are not followed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
brown eyes, and a somewhat round face.

Not sure what you'd replace this with but somewhat round face doesn't fit in with clinical tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
014, conc boy!
This is one of the best articles of the first series. Simple, to the point, memorable. You just know that this guy is going to be around forever, just kinda sitting there. The note at the end judt tues the whole thing together. Probably the easiest upvote so far.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do luke simplicity most of the time, and I suppose simplicity is necessitated by this particular humanoid SCP object, but I would like to know more about the guys circumstances.

This article has a nice explanation for the phenomena at the end, but I would also like to see some of that reasoning more present before the end.
Again though… The simplicity necessitates brevity, so it's not any inconvenience…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably a very good thing he's happy to just sit and listen to music really…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …So what would happen if he and SCP-239 were put in the same room together? They appear to have the same type of ability, except they took complete different directions with it, where SCP-014 caused himself to be immutable, and she causes everything else to change on her whims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His abilities aren't a placebo effect. In fact, they're the complete opposite of a placebo effect - they're taking the wording of cogito ergo sum, "I think, therefore I am", and being as literal as possible with it.

While placebos only make you think something works when it most certainly does not work on its own merits, his thinking appears to have forced reality to act on the merit of his own thinking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the final addendum at the bottom kinda makes it seem like his abilities are a placebo effect. I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm over-thinking it but it seems like that little note at the end implies that this SCP has the potential to be more powerful and therefore dangerous than currently believed.
By sheer willpower it believes it is concrete and can live forever which is rather mundane, but what if it had convinced itself of something else like super strength, super speed, maybe even flight? Etc.

item.title: Brilliant
item.content:encoded:
I'm new here but this scp is one of the best I have read! The simplicity makes it so good! It's just a man who thinks he's immortal and stone! Proof that good scps doesn't have to be creepy or scary. It makes you wonder if the curse is real or is his brain stopping the functions of the body because he really does believe he's immortal and stone.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: basically how i felt too, i think it was the ending that made me upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't really fond of this guy at first, but he grew on me. There's something ridiculously endearing about him and the fact that all he does is sit motionless all day and is apparently immortal due to his own delusions. +1 for the precious concrete dork.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, this is something that caught me off guard. I wasn't expecting something like this to be this good! It's simplicity and brevity attracts me for some reason. This is a great article for me, and it honestly holds up well together today in my opinion today. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great SCP but it seems very similar to SCP-138 the ever living man. Both rarely move if at all and, both have been alive for way longer than they should've been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addendum to the above, keep in mind that the man is still aware. It's not as if he's completely in his own world. Was he literally just a chunk of concrete who was unaware of the world around him the containment procedures might be unnecessary but as it is, the least you can do is entertain the poor guy. An iPod playing classical music is quite possibly the most simplistic containment measure I've ever seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well…. pffffaaa/…

I'm going to say it's almost certainly public domain, then. Published in the USA pre-1923 = public domain. The pic is kind of sepia-toned, and Whitewings wanted someone "of the appropriate era" so we can reasonably assume it was from the 1910s…

and if it wasn't published before 1923, but was just a family photo kept in a box somewhere until it was scanned and put online, then there's https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Template:PD-US-unpublished which could apply…

so I'm going to put 90% odds on this image being public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, and this entire time, I thought that he was a block of concrete and that the picture wa of normal quality and that the background was just the concrete.

After re-reading this…I find my original misconception to be more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eight months late, but does it really inconvenience to Foundation at all to have a music player somewhere in the room with a few MP3s on loop, a chair, and a window? Given how much they're willing to spend on other SCPs just to learn minor details about how they work, this is more than reasonable to keep him relatively quiet and happy. The style guide says something along the lines of "the Foundation is cold, not cruel;" this fits that description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the idea, love the researcher's note, hate the containment procedures. Why are we giving this guy anything? He "wants" nothing more than to exist as concrete. And he needs nothing to do that. I wouldn't care if music was at all significant to him but it doesn't appear to be.

But considering its +206 rating, my minor quibble hardly matters haha. And I like the idea enough to not down vote based on my reservations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea. I just performed an image search for a man of the appropriate era.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder who that is in the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now, almost six months later, it's up to +115. This, ladies and gentlemen, is how an article ages gracefully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking butt ghost is higher than that. It means nothing nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only that it's the current article rating, and last I checked this article it was much, much lower. In the 20s or maybe 30s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it is a good article early on in the series, one of the first ones that people see if they start from the beginning. It's not mind-blowing, but definitely worth an upvote and a place in anyone's headcanon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something significant about 73?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck

WHEN DID THIS BREAK +73

I mean, I'm not complaining, I've always liked this one, but whaaaaaaaaat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perfect old article, this is how I think a lot of articles should be. I can think of a shitload of new articles that could really, really use a trimming to something around this length.

Also holy shit it's gone up bu 50 votes since I last saw it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to agree with the general consensus; the note at the end really brings life to this. It shows genuine curiosity and interest on the part of the researcher, rather than the clinical detachment typical of SCP articles. Maybe it's not exceptionally professional sounding, but in a case like this, how could you not be interested?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one really makes me think, and I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I think it would be better if it had some more detail to it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. It adds a really strong note of realism to it, in my opinion. Obviously researchers deal with a lot of crazy shit, but this is awfully poignant, even to the point of giving professionalism a little poke in the eye.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels incomplete compared to other reports. Has there been any testing of his skin to see if it is truly turning to concrete or has any other biochemical changes? Are his clothes still intact, do his clothes have to be replaced, or is he naked? The researchers seem to have fallen down on the job in SCP-014's case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know, in spite of the rigid professionalism that is Foundation SOP, the hint of romanticism does feel like a slip of the tongue; i.e. that it's the kind of comment a researcher would make in that given circumstance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…apart from his eyes and vocal apparatus, is to all appearances utterly immobile…"

So he probably blinks. And we don't mix SCPs because of unforeseen effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That seems like a very good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slightly annoyed by the romanticism of the researcher note, but besides that, this is pretty good. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If he is turning into a statue, and he can't move, will placing him in front of 173 keep it from moving?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not suggesting a cross-experimentation log but I'd think that he'd get along fine with good ol' 176.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am very late to the party, but I have to say, job well done, my good man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think we should test his commitment to being concrete with a section of rebar and his [DATA EXPUNGED]
